message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 19
Recent Green phase: "cursed sword + plain amulet → premium 231" passed immediately, confirming that item-specific surcharges (curse) apply to the affected item's base while policy-wide modifiers (first-insurance) apply to the policy base, on a multi-item policy. No code change this cycle.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. Do not implement claim. If already clean, document why.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 19 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the code for refactoring opportunities.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

I reviewed all the public and helper names against the latest test ("cursed sword + plain amulet → premium 231"):

- `quote` — clearly the public entry point. Fits.
- `policyBasePremium`, `mainItemsBasePremium`, `componentsBasePremium` — accurately describe the base premium decomposition. Fit.
- `itemSurcharges` / `policyWideModifiers` — the two-tier structure (item-specific vs policy-wide) is exactly what the latest test exercises. Names reveal intent well, and the tier comments reinforce it.
- `surchargeWhen` / `fractionOfPolicyBaseWhen` — generic helpers parameterizing "fraction of a base when a condition holds." Clear.
- `roundUpInMHPCOsFavor`, `componentGroupBasePremium`, `countByType`, `sumOf` — all descriptive and accurate.

Naming is already strong and matches the current full intent. No rename needed.

## Step 2: APP Mass — candidate spots

I looked for the highest-value structural improvement. The strongest candidate is `countByType`, the only remaining imperative mutation-heavy helper:

```typescript
const countByType = (items: Item[]): Map<string, number> => {
  const counts = new Map<string, number>();          // binding(1) + invocation Map(2) = 3
  for (const item of items) {                          // loop(5) + binding item(1) = 6
    counts.set(item.type, (counts.get(item.type) ?? 0) + 1);  // set(2)+get(2)+ +(2) + bindings counts,item.type + const 1
  }
  return counts;
};
```

This helper carries a loop (5), two mutating-style `Map` operations per iteration, and an explicit assignment-via-`set`. Mass roughly: bindings ~4, constants 1, invocations 4 (`new Map`, `set`, `get`, `+`), loop 5 ≈ **18**.

Everything else in the file is already declarative (built on `sumOf`, ternaries, and small pure helpers). `sumOf` itself is the one sanctioned imperative primitive that all the declarative code reuses, so keeping its loop localized is good design (Rule 3 — single representation of "fold over an iterable").

`countByType` is the one place that re-implements an imperative loop *outside* `sumOf`. However, reducing it (e.g. `items.reduce(...)` building a Map) would introduce an assignment-heavy reducer that isn't clearly lower mass and would hurt readability (Rule 2 trumps APP). The imperative `for...of` here is actually the clearest expression.

## Step 3: The real improvement — Rule 3 (No Duplication) in `policyBasePremium`

Looking at `policyBasePremium`:

```typescript
const policyBasePremium = (items: Item[]): number =>
  mainItemsBasePremium(items.filter(isMainItem)) +
  componentsBasePremium(items.filter(isComponent));
```

This filters `items` **twice** and pairs each filter with its matching pricing function. There's a conceptual coupling: "main items are everything that isn't a component" is already expressed by `isMainItem = !isComponent`. The double-filter is a mild duplication of the partitioning concept, but splitting into main/component is genuinely two different pricing strategies, so two passes is clear and correct. Collapsing it would obscure intent. I'll leave it.

Let me look once more for a concrete, safe win. The pair `isMainItem`/`isComponent` and the two filter calls are fine. The most defensible single refactoring is in `surchargeWhen` and `fractionOfPolicyBaseWhen`: `firstInsuranceSurcharge` calls `fractionOfPolicyBaseWhen(policyBase, true, rate)` with a hardcoded `true` — a degenerate conditional.

121	  fractionOfPolicyBaseWhen(

content.text:
## Step 4: Implement the refactoring

`firstInsuranceSurcharge` always applies — it passes `applies: true` to `fractionOfPolicyBaseWhen`, which then evaluates a conditional (`true ? ... : 0`) that can never take the false branch. This is a degenerate conditional: it adds APP mass (a conditional, mass 4) and slightly obscures intent ("when does it apply? always"). The first-insurance surcharge is unconditional per the spec, so expressing it directly as `rate * policyBase` is both clearer (Rule 2) and lower mass (Rule 4 — removes the unnecessary conditional element).

**Before** (`firstInsuranceSurcharge` path):
- `fractionOfPolicyBaseWhen(policyBase, true, rate)`: invocation(2) + constant `true`(1) + bindings(2) + conditional(4) + invocation `*`(2) = **~11**

**After** (direct): `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE * policyBase`: invocation `*`(2) + bindings(2) = **~4**. Removes one constant and one conditional (Δ mass −7 on this path).

content.text:
All 19 tests still pass.

## Refactoring Complete

**Naming Evaluation (done first):** All names — `quote`, `policyBasePremium`, `mainItemsBasePremium`, `componentsBasePremium`, `itemSurcharges`, `policyWideModifiers`, `surchargeWhen`, `fractionOfPolicyBaseWhen`, `roundUpInMHPCOsFavor` — accurately reveal intent and match the current full behavior exercised by the latest "cursed sword + plain amulet → 231" test (which validates the two-tier item-specific vs policy-wide modifier split). No rename needed.

**Refactoring Applied (Rule 4 — Fewest Elements; supports Rule 2 — Reveals Intent):**
- `firstInsuranceSurcharge` was routing through `fractionOfPolicyBaseWhen(policyBase, true, rate)`, which evaluates a conditional whose false branch is unreachable (`applies` is hardcoded `true`). The first-insurance surcharge is unconditional per spec.
- Changed to the direct expression `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE * policyBase`.

**APP Mass (this path):**
- Before: invocation `fractionOfPolicyBaseWhen`(2) + constant `true`(1) + conditional(4) + invocation `*`(2) + bindings(2) ≈ **11**
- After: invocation `*`(2) + bindings(2) ≈ **4**
- Δ ≈ **−7**; removed one constant and one (dead) conditional.

**Why other candidates were left alone:**
- `countByType` keeps an imperative `for...of`, but converting it to a `reduce` building a Map would add an assignment-heavy reducer with no clear mass win and worse readability — Rule 2 trumps APP.
- The double `items.filter(...)` in `policyBasePremium` is two distinct pricing strategies (main items vs components), so two passes reads clearly; collapsing it would obscure intent.
- `fractionOfPolicyBaseWhen` is still correctly used by `loyaltyDiscount` and `followUpDiscount`, which genuinely have conditions — the helper stays.

**Tests:** All 19 passing (20 todo untouched). `claim` was not implemented, as instructed.
